questions.questionText: A patient with chronic steatorrhea is found to have deficiency of essential fatty acids. The most specific clinical manifestation differentiating EFA deficiency from other nutritional deficiencies is:
questions.optionB: Scaly dermatitis (phrynoderma), impaired wound healing, and elevated triene-to-tetraene ratio (Mead acid/arachidonic acid >0.2) — as the body substitutes 5,8,11-eicosatrienoic acid (Mead acid) for absent arachidonate
questions.explanation: Essential fatty acid deficiency — biochemical diagnosis: EFAs: linoleic acid (18:2 ω-6, parent of arachidonic acid) and α-linolenic acid (18:3 ω-3, parent of EPA/DHA). Cannot be synthesized by humans (lack Δ12 and Δ15 desaturases). Deficiency: (1) Scaly dermatitis (phrynoderma), (2) Poor wound healing, (3) Growth retardation, (4) Increased susceptibility to infection. Biochemical marker: TRIENE/TETRAENE RATIO (Holman index): in EFA deficiency, body synthesizes MEAD ACID (5,8,11-eicosatrienoic acid, a ω-9 fatty acid from oleate using same elongation/desaturation enzymes). Ratio of Mead acid/arachidonic acid >0.2 = diagnostic of EFA deficiency. Clinical: seen in TPN without lipid emulsion, fat malabsorption, severe fat restriction. Reference: Harper's — EFA Deficiency.
questions.conceptTag: EFA deficiency triene tetraene Mead acid

questions.questionText: Cholesterol cannot be degraded to CO2 and H2O in humans. The ONLY significant route of cholesterol elimination from the body is:
questions.optionB: Conversion to bile acids (primary: cholic and chenodeoxycholic acid) by CYP7A1 in the liver, followed by fecal excretion of bile acids and free cholesterol — this is why bile acid sequestrants lower cholesterol
questions.explanation: Cholesterol elimination: Humans CANNOT break the steroid ring → cholesterol is not oxidized for energy. Elimination routes: (1) BILE ACID synthesis (major ~500mg/day): CYP7A1 (7α-hydroxylase, rate-limiting, ER) → primary bile acids (cholate, chenodeoxycholate). Conjugated with glycine/taurine → bile salts → secreted into bile → intestine → 95% reabsorbed (enterohepatic circulation). ~5% lost in feces = NET cholesterol elimination. (2) Fecal cholesterol: unabsorbed biliary cholesterol + dietary cholesterol. (3) Minor: cholesterol → steroid hormones, vitamin D precursor (small quantities). Pharmacological exploitation: bile acid sequestrants (cholestyramine) → bind bile acids in gut → prevent reabsorption → liver must synthesize more bile acids from cholesterol → LDL-R upregulation → lowers plasma cholesterol. Reference: Harper's — Cholesterol Elimination.

questions.questionText: Sphingomyelin is composed of:
questions.optionB: Ceramide (sphingosine + fatty acid) + phosphocholine
questions.explanation: Sphingomyelin = ceramide + phosphocholine. Ceramide = sphingosine + fatty acid (amide bond). Sphingomyelin is found in myelin sheaths (hence the name) and RBC membranes. Niemann-Pick disease (Type A): sphingomyelinase deficiency → sphingomyelin accumulation (cherry-red spot on macula, hepatosplenomegaly, foam cells). Sphingolipidoses are lysosomal storage diseases. Reference: Harper's — Sphingolipids.

questions.questionText: Gangliosides contain which unique sugar component?
questions.optionB: Sialic acid (NANA/N-acetylneuraminic acid)
questions.explanation: Gangliosides: ceramide + oligosaccharide containing one or more sialic acid (NANA) residues. Abundant in brain gray matter, nerve endings. GM1 = 1 sialic acid, GD = 2, GT = 3. Tay-Sachs disease: hexosaminidase A deficiency → GM2 ganglioside accumulation → cherry-red spot on macula, progressive neurodegeneration, death by age 3-4. Gaucher's disease: glucocerebrosidase deficiency → glucocerebroside accumulation. Reference: Harper's — Gangliosides.

questions.questionText: HMG-CoA reductase is regulated at MULTIPLE levels simultaneously. Which combination of regulatory mechanisms is correct?
questions.optionB: Transcriptional (SREBP-2 activation when cholesterol low), translational (Insig-mediated mRNA degradation), post-translational (phosphorylation by AMPK = inactive, dephosphorylation = active), AND proteolytic (sterol-induced ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation)
questions.explanation: HMG-CoA reductase — multi-level regulation (most regulated enzyme in body): (1) TRANSCRIPTIONAL: SREBP-2 (sterol regulatory element binding protein): when cholesterol LOW → SCAP escorts SREBP-2 from ER to Golgi → proteolytic cleavage (S1P, S2P) → active SREBP-2 → nucleus → activates HMG-CoA reductase gene. When cholesterol HIGH: Insig retains SCAP-SREBP in ER → no activation. (2) POST-TRANSLATIONAL phosphorylation: AMPK (AMP-activated kinase, low energy) → PHOSPHORYLATES → INACTIVE. Protein phosphatase → dephosphorylates → ACTIVE. (3) PROTEOLYTIC: high sterol levels → Insig binds sterol-sensing domain → ubiquitination → proteasomal degradation (decreased enzyme mass). (4) DRUG: Statins: competitive inhibitors (structural analogs of HMG-CoA). Reference: Harper's — HMG-CoA Reductase Regulation.

questions.questionText: Phosphatidylcholine (lecithin) is the most abundant phospholipid and its main function is:
questions.optionB: Major structural component of cell membranes and lung surfactant (DPPC)
questions.explanation: Phosphatidylcholine (lecithin): most abundant phospholipid in cell membranes (outer leaflet). Dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC) is the main component of lung surfactant. Also important for bile (mixed micelles), lipoproteins, and as source of arachidonic acid (via phospholipase A2). LCAT (lecithin-cholesterol acyltransferase) transfers fatty acid from lecithin to cholesterol → cholesterol ester (HDL maturation). Reference: Harper's — Phospholipids.

questions.questionText: Lipoproteins that transport endogenous triglycerides from the liver to peripheral tissues are:
questions.optionB: VLDL (very low-density lipoproteins)
questions.explanation: VLDL: endogenous TG transport (liver → peripheral tissues). Chylomicrons: exogenous (dietary) TG transport (intestine → tissues). LDL: cholesterol delivery to tissues ('bad cholesterol'). HDL: reverse cholesterol transport (tissues → liver, 'good cholesterol'). VLDL → (lipoprotein lipase removes TG) → IDL → LDL. ApoB-100 on VLDL/LDL. ApoB-48 on chylomicrons. ApoC-II activates LPL. Reference: Harper's — Lipoproteins.

questions.questionText: Tay-Sachs disease is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Hexosaminidase A
questions.explanation: Tay-Sachs disease: hexosaminidase A deficiency → GM2 ganglioside accumulation (neurons). AR, common in Ashkenazi Jews. Presentation: progressive neurodegeneration, cherry-red spot on macula (ganglion cells around fovea accumulate lipid, fovea appears red), blindness, seizures, death by age 3-4. No hepatosplenomegaly (unlike Niemann-Pick). Gaucher: glucocerebrosidase. Niemann-Pick A: sphingomyelinase. Fabry: α-galactosidase A. Reference: Harper's — Sphingolipidoses.

questions.questionText: Prostaglandins and thromboxanes have opposing effects on platelet aggregation. The biochemical basis for low-dose aspirin's antiplatelet effect (but not anti-inflammatory at low dose) is:
questions.optionB: Low-dose aspirin irreversibly acetylates COX-1 in platelets (which lack nuclei and cannot resynthesize COX) → TxA2 production permanently blocked for platelet lifespan (~7-10 days); endothelial cells (nucleated) can resynthesize COX → PGI2 production recovers
questions.explanation: Low-dose aspirin pharmacology: Aspirin: IRREVERSIBLE ACETYLATION of Ser530 in COX-1. Platelets: ANUCLEATE → cannot transcribe new COX-1 mRNA → COX-1 permanently inactivated for entire platelet lifespan (7-10 days). Result: NO thromboxane A2 (TxA2, potent vasoconstrictor + platelet aggregator). Endothelial cells: NUCLEATED → can resynthesize COX → PGI2 (prostacyclin, vasodilator + anti-aggregator) production RECOVERS between doses. Net effect of LOW dose (75-100mg): TxA2 ↓↓↓ (permanent) but PGI2 recovery → ANTIPLATELET without anti-inflammatory effect. HIGH dose (>300mg): inhibits both platelet COX-1 AND endothelial COX-2 → anti-inflammatory but also blocks PGI2 → less net antiplatelet benefit. This explains the 'aspirin paradox.' Reference: Harper's — Low-Dose Aspirin.
questions.conceptTag: Aspirin COX-1 irreversible platelet TxA2 PGI2

questions.questionText: De novo fatty acid synthesis occurs in the cytoplasm using NADPH. The two major sources of cytoplasmic NADPH for lipogenesis are:
questions.optionB: The HMP shunt (pentose phosphate pathway, via G6PD and 6PGDH) and the malic enzyme (converts malate → pyruvate + CO2 + NADPH, part of the citrate shuttle)
questions.optionC: ETC Complex I and Complex III
questions.explanation: NADPH sources for fatty acid synthesis: FA synthesis requires 14 NADPH per palmitate (7 cycles × 2 NADPH). Source 1: HMP SHUNT (pentose phosphate pathway) — oxidative phase: G6P → 6PG → ribulose-5-P, generating 2 NADPH per glucose. Account for ~60% of lipogenic NADPH. Source 2: MALIC ENZYME (cytoplasmic): part of citrate shuttle — OAA is reduced to malate (by cytoplasmic malate DH, consuming NADH), then malate → pyruvate + CO2 by MALIC ENZYME, generating NADPH. Accounts for ~40%. Citrate shuttle overview: mitochondrial acetyl-CoA + OAA → citrate (citrate synthase) → citrate exits via citrate transporter → ATP-citrate lyase cleaves → acetyl-CoA (for FA synthesis) + OAA → malate → (malic enzyme) → pyruvate + NADPH. Reference: Harper's — NADPH Lipogenesis.
questions.conceptTag: NADPH sources HMP malic enzyme lipogenesis

questions.questionText: The iodine value of a dietary fat is clinically relevant because:
questions.optionB: Higher iodine value indicates more C=C double bonds (more unsaturated) — polyunsaturated fats (high IV) are more prone to lipid peroxidation (rancidity) and free radical generation, relevant to antioxidant requirements and shelf life
questions.optionC: It measures iodine content of fat
questions.explanation: Iodine value — clinical and nutritional chemistry: Definition: grams of I2 absorbed per 100g of fat. I2 adds across C=C double bonds → more double bonds = higher IV. Saturated fats: IV ~10 (butter ~35, coconut oil ~10). Monounsaturated: IV ~80-100 (olive oil ~85). Polyunsaturated: IV >100 (soybean ~130, linseed ~180). Clinical significance: (1) Oxidative stability: higher IV → more double bonds → more susceptible to LIPID PEROXIDATION (free radical chain reaction: initiation → propagation → termination) → produces MDA, 4-HNE → cell damage, atherogenesis. (2) Higher IV fats require more vitamin E (antioxidant). (3) Hydrogenation reduces IV (adds H across double bonds) but creates TRANS fats. (4) Acid value: measures free fatty acid content (rancidity indicator). Saponification value: molecular weight indicator. Reference: Harper's — Lipid Chemistry Tests.

questions.questionText: LDL receptor-mediated endocytosis is defective in:
questions.optionB: Familial hypercholesterolemia (Type IIa)
questions.explanation: Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH, Type IIa): mutations in LDL receptor (or ApoB-100 or PCSK9) → defective LDL uptake → markedly elevated serum LDL cholesterol → premature atherosclerosis. Heterozygous (1:500): LDL ~300-400 mg/dL, tendon xanthomas. Homozygous (1:1 million): LDL >600 mg/dL, childhood MI. Treatment: statins, PCSK9 inhibitors. Tangier: ABCA1 deficiency → low HDL. Reference: Harper's — FH.

questions.questionText: Cerebrosides are:
questions.optionB: Ceramide + single sugar (galactose or glucose)
questions.explanation: Cerebrosides: simplest glycosphingolipid = ceramide + one sugar. Galactocerebrosides: abundant in brain myelin. Glucocerebrosides: non-neural tissues. Gaucher disease: glucocerebrosidase (β-glucosidase) deficiency → glucocerebroside accumulation → Gaucher cells (crinkled tissue paper cytoplasm). Most common lysosomal storage disease. Type 1 (non-neuronopathic): hepatosplenomegaly, bone disease. Treatment: ERT (imiglucerase). Reference: Harper's — Cerebrosides.

questions.questionText: The fluid mosaic model is updated by the 'lipid raft' hypothesis. Lipid rafts are important because:
questions.optionB: They are cholesterol- and sphingolipid-rich microdomains that are more ordered (liquid-ordered phase) than bulk membrane, serving as platforms for concentrating signaling proteins (GPI-anchored, Src kinases) and facilitating signal transduction
questions.optionC: They contain only proteins without lipids
questions.optionD: They are found only in bacteria
questions.explanation: Lipid rafts — updated membrane model: Singer-Nicolson (1972): fluid mosaic model — proteins floating in homogeneous lipid bilayer. Lipid rafts (current model): MICRODOMAINS enriched in: (1) Cholesterol: fills gaps between sphingolipid hydrocarbon chains → tighter packing → LIQUID-ORDERED phase (more ordered than surrounding membrane but still fluid), (2) Sphingolipids: long saturated acyl chains → pack tightly with cholesterol. Raft functions: (1) Concentrate signaling molecules (GPI-anchored proteins, Src family kinases, G-proteins) → enhance signal transduction, (2) Pathogen entry (HIV gp41, influenza HA use rafts), (3) Caveolae: specialized invaginated rafts (caveolin protein) → endocytosis, transcytosis, (4) Cancer: altered raft composition affects drug resistance. Detergent-resistant membranes (DRMs) at 4°C is experimental tool. Reference: Harper's — Lipid Rafts.
questions.conceptTag: Lipid rafts cholesterol sphingolipid signaling platforms

questions.questionText: Industrial trans fatty acid consumption increases cardiovascular risk through a specific mechanism at the lipoprotein level:
questions.optionB: Trans fats simultaneously INCREASE LDL (by decreasing LDL receptor activity) AND DECREASE HDL (by increasing CETP activity), creating a doubly atherogenic profile — worse than saturated fats which only increase LDL
questions.explanation: Trans fatty acids — cardiovascular mechanism: Industrial TFAs (partial hydrogenation of vegetable oils): elaidic acid (trans-18:1 Δ9) is the major dietary TFA. Effects on lipoproteins: (1) INCREASE LDL: TFAs downregulate LDL receptor expression (possibly via altered SREBP processing) → decreased LDL clearance, (2) DECREASE HDL: increase CETP (cholesteryl ester transfer protein) activity → increased CE transfer from HDL to VLDL/LDL. (3) Increase Lp(a). (4) Promote inflammation (increase IL-6, TNF-α, CRP). (5) Promote endothelial dysfunction (decrease NO). Net effect: WORSE than saturated fats (which increase both LDL AND HDL). WHO recommendation: <1% of total energy from TFAs. FDA ban on PHOs (partially hydrogenated oils) in 2018. Natural TFAs (ruminant: vaccenic acid, CLA) may be less harmful. Reference: Harper's — Trans Fatty Acids.
questions.conceptTag: Trans fats LDL increase HDL decrease CETP

questions.questionText: Gaucher disease is the most common lysosomal storage disease, caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Glucocerebrosidase (β-glucosidase)
questions.explanation: Gaucher disease: glucocerebrosidase deficiency → glucocerebroside accumulation in macrophages (Gaucher cells with 'crinkled tissue paper' cytoplasm). Type 1 (most common, non-neuronopathic): hepatosplenomegaly, pancytopenia, bone crises (Erlenmeyer flask deformity). Type 2 (acute neuronopathic): fatal in infancy. Type 3 (subacute neuronopathic). Treatment: enzyme replacement therapy (imiglucerase), substrate reduction (miglustat). Reference: Harper's — Gaucher.

questions.questionText: Cardiolipin (diphosphatidylglycerol) is found predominantly in:
questions.optionB: Inner mitochondrial membrane
questions.explanation: Cardiolipin: unique phospholipid found almost exclusively in the inner mitochondrial membrane. Structure: two phosphatidic acid molecules linked by glycerol → 4 fatty acid chains. Essential for electron transport chain complexes (especially Complex III and IV) and ATP synthase function. Antibodies against cardiolipin are found in antiphospholipid syndrome (lupus anticoagulant) → thrombosis, recurrent miscarriages. VDRL test (syphilis) uses cardiolipin. Reference: Harper's — Cardiolipin.

questions.questionText: Fabry disease is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: α-galactosidase A
questions.explanation: Fabry disease: α-galactosidase A deficiency → globotriaosylceramide (Gb3) accumulation. X-linked recessive (only sphingolipidosis that is X-linked). Features: acroparesthesias (burning pain in hands/feet), angiokeratomas (skin lesions), corneal dystrophy (cornea verticillata), renal failure, cardiac disease. Maltese cross bodies in urine. Treatment: enzyme replacement (agalsidase alfa/beta). Reference: Harper's — Fabry Disease.

questions.questionText: In diabetic ketoacidosis, the ratio of β-hydroxybutyrate to acetoacetate is significantly elevated (normally 1:1, in DKA up to 10:1). The biochemical basis for this shift is:
questions.optionB: Excess NADH from hepatic fatty acid β-oxidation drives β-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase toward β-hydroxybutyrate formation (acetoacetate + NADH → β-hydroxybutyrate + NAD+), reflecting the high mitochondrial NADH/NAD+ ratio
questions.optionD: Acetoacetate spontaneously converts completely to acetone
questions.explanation: Ketone body ratio in DKA: Ketogenesis (liver mitochondria): Acetyl-CoA → acetoacetyl-CoA → HMG-CoA → acetoacetate (by HMG-CoA lyase). Acetoacetate ⇌ β-hydroxybutyrate (by β-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase, uses NADH/NAD+). Acetoacetate → acetone (spontaneous decarboxylation, minor). In DKA: massive β-oxidation → excess FADH2 and NADH → mitochondrial NADH/NAD+ ratio VERY HIGH → equilibrium SHIFTS toward β-hydroxybutyrate. Ratio β-HB:AcAc rises from 1:1 to up to 10:1. Clinical consequence: standard urine ketone test (nitroprusside) detects only ACETOACETATE (not β-HB) → may UNDERESTIMATE true ketosis severity! Serum β-hydroxybutyrate measurement is preferred. During treatment: as NADH/NAD+ normalizes, β-HB converts to AcAc → urine ketones may INCREASE despite clinical improvement (paradoxical). Reference: Harper's — Ketone Body Ratio.

questions.questionText: Carnitine shuttle is required for transport of:
questions.optionB: Long-chain fatty acyl-CoA into mitochondrial matrix for β-oxidation
questions.explanation: Carnitine shuttle: long-chain fatty acyl-CoA cannot cross inner mitochondrial membrane directly. CPT-I (outer membrane, rate-limiting): acyl-CoA + carnitine → acylcarnitine. Carnitine-acylcarnitine translocase: shuttles acylcarnitine across. CPT-II (inner membrane): acylcarnitine → acyl-CoA + carnitine (regenerated). CPT-I is inhibited by malonyl-CoA (product of ACC — prevents simultaneous synthesis and oxidation). Medium/short-chain FAs don't need carnitine shuttle. Reference: Harper's — Carnitine Shuttle.

questions.questionText: LDL is the primary delivery vehicle for cholesterol to peripheral tissues. The mechanism of LDL clearance involves:
questions.optionB: Receptor-mediated endocytosis: ApoB-100 on LDL binds LDL receptor → clathrin-coated pit internalization → endosome → CURL (uncoupling of receptor and ligand) → receptor recycled, LDL → lysosome → cholesterol released → downregulates SREBP-2 and HMG-CoA reductase
questions.explanation: LDL receptor pathway (Brown and Goldstein, Nobel 1985): (1) LDL binds LDL-R via ApoB-100 recognition, (2) Internalized in CLATHRIN-coated pits (adaptin, dynamin), (3) Clathrin released (by auxilin/Hsc70), vesicle → early endosome, (4) CURL (compartment of uncoupling of receptor and ligand): pH 5.5 → LDL dissociates from receptor, (5) Receptor RECYCLED to surface (~150 trips), (6) LDL → lysosome: cholesterol esters hydrolyzed by acid lipase → FREE cholesterol. Regulatory responses to intracellular cholesterol: (1) ↓SREBP-2 activity → ↓HMG-CoA reductase transcription, (2) ↑ACAT (acyl-CoA:cholesterol acyltransferase) → re-esterification for storage, (3) ↓LDL receptor expression (prevents over-accumulation). FH: LDL-R mutations → impaired clearance → high plasma LDL. PCSK9: promotes LDL-R degradation. Reference: Harper's — LDL Receptor Pathway.
questions.conceptTag: LDL receptor endocytosis CURL SREBP regulation

questions.questionText: Niemann-Pick disease Type A is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Sphingomyelinase (acid sphingomyelinase)
questions.explanation: Niemann-Pick Type A: acid sphingomyelinase deficiency → sphingomyelin accumulation. Presentation: hepatosplenomegaly (foam cells), cherry-red spot on macula, progressive neurodegeneration, death by age 3. Type B: similar enzyme deficiency but milder (no CNS involvement). Type C: NPC1/NPC2 mutations (cholesterol trafficking defect) → progressive neurodegeneration, vertical supranuclear gaze palsy. Krabbe: galactocerebrosidase. MLD: arylsulfatase A. Reference: Harper's — Niemann-Pick.

questions.questionText: Metachromatic leukodystrophy (MLD) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Arylsulfatase A (cerebroside sulfatase)
questions.explanation: MLD: arylsulfatase A deficiency → sulfatide (cerebroside sulfate) accumulation in white matter → progressive demyelination. AR inheritance. Sulfatides stain metachromatically (brown with cresyl violet instead of expected purple → hence 'metachromatic'). Progressive neurological deterioration. Diagnosed by arylsulfatase A activity in leukocytes. Krabbe disease: galactocerebrosidase → galactocerebroside → globoid cells. Reference: Harper's — MLD.

questions.questionText: Arachidonic acid is biochemically most important as a precursor of:
questions.optionB: Eicosanoids (prostaglandins, thromboxanes, leukotrienes)
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Arachidonic acid (20:4, omega-6) is released from membrane phospholipids and converted to eicosanoids by COX and LOX pathways.

questions.questionText: Which lipoprotein is richest in triglycerides in fasting plasma?
questions.optionB: VLDL
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. In fasting state, VLDL is the major triglyceride-rich lipoprotein. Chylomicrons dominate post-prandially.

questions.questionText: LCAT in plasma is mainly associated with:
questions.optionB: HDL-mediated reverse cholesterol transport
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. LCAT esterifies free cholesterol on HDL, helping maturation of HDL particles and reverse cholesterol transport.

questions.questionText: Plasmalogens differ from regular phospholipids in that they contain:
questions.optionB: A vinyl ether bond at sn-1 position (instead of ester bond)
questions.explanation: Plasmalogens: glycerophospholipids with a vinyl ETHER bond (not ester) at sn-1 carbon (α,β-unsaturated ether linkage). Abundant in: heart, brain, and immune cells. Types: ethanolamine plasmalogen (most common) and choline plasmalogen. Function: antioxidant (vinyl ether bond scavenges ROS), membrane structure, cell signaling. Deficiency: Zellweger syndrome (peroxisomal biogenesis disorder) — plasmalogens are synthesized starting in peroxisomes (initial steps) then completed in ER. Reference: Harper's — Plasmalogens.

questions.questionText: Bile acid sequestrants (cholestyramine, colesevelam) are used in combination with statins. The synergistic mechanism involves:
questions.optionB: Sequestrants bind bile acids in the gut → interrupt enterohepatic circulation → liver diverts more cholesterol to bile acid synthesis (via CYP7A1 upregulation) → cholesterol depletion → LDL-R upregulation; statins independently block cholesterol synthesis → additive LDL-R upregulation → greater LDL clearance
questions.optionC: Sequestrants absorb statins for better delivery
questions.explanation: Bile acid sequestrant + statin synergy: Normal enterohepatic circulation: ~95% of bile acids reabsorbed (ileum, ASBT transporter) → return to liver via portal vein → recycled. Sequestrants: positively charged resins bind NEGATIVELY charged bile acids → fecal excretion → interrupted enterohepatic circulation → liver loses bile acids → compensatory upregulation of CYP7A1 → MORE cholesterol → bile acids → hepatocyte cholesterol DEPLETION → SREBP-2 activated → LDL-R upregulation. Side effect: CYP7A1 upregulation also activates HMG-CoA reductase (compensatory cholesterol synthesis) → limits LDL lowering (~15-20%). Adding STATIN: blocks compensatory HMG-CoA reductase increase → synergistic LDL-R upregulation → 50-60% LDL reduction combined. Reference: Harper's — Bile Acid Sequestrants.
questions.conceptTag: Bile acid sequestrant statin synergy enterohepatic

questions.questionText: Platelet-activating factor (PAF) is a modified phospholipid with an:
questions.optionB: Ether bond at sn-1 and acetyl group at sn-2 (shortest fatty acid chain on a phospholipid)
questions.explanation: PAF (1-O-alkyl-2-acetyl-sn-glycero-3-phosphocholine): unique phospholipid with ether linkage at sn-1 (alkyl group) and very short acetyl group at sn-2 (instead of typical long-chain fatty acid). One of the most potent biological mediators. Functions: platelet aggregation, inflammation, neutrophil activation, bronchoconstriction, hypotension (at picomolar concentrations). Released by: basophils, neutrophils, monocytes, endothelial cells, platelets. Inactivated by PAF-acetylhydrolase. Reference: Harper's — PAF.

questions.questionText: Lung surfactant is predominantly composed of:
questions.optionB: Dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (DPPC/dipalmitoyl lecithin)
questions.explanation: Lung surfactant: secreted by Type II pneumocytes (alveolar cells). Composition: ~80% phospholipids (mainly DPPC — dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine), ~10% surfactant proteins (SP-A, SP-B, SP-C, SP-D), ~10% other lipids. DPPC reduces alveolar surface tension → prevents alveolar collapse (prevents atelectasis). L/S ratio (lecithin/sphingomyelin) > 2.0 in amniotic fluid indicates fetal lung maturity. Deficiency: Respiratory Distress Syndrome (RDS) of newborn (hyaline membrane disease) — premature neonates. Treatment: exogenous surfactant replacement. Reference: Harper's — Lung Surfactant.

questions.questionText: Cholesterol ester transfer protein (CETP) functions to:
questions.optionB: Transfer cholesterol esters from HDL to VLDL/LDL in exchange for triglycerides
questions.optionC: Transport cholesterol to adrenal glands only
questions.explanation: CETP: plasma protein that mediates exchange/transfer of cholesterol esters (CE) from HDL to VLDL/LDL in exchange for triglycerides. This is part of reverse cholesterol transport. CETP activity: increases LDL-C and decreases HDL-C. CETP deficiency: very high HDL levels. CETP inhibitors (anacetrapib, dalcetrapib): developed to raise HDL — mixed clinical trial results. LCAT: esterifies free cholesterol on HDL surface (lecithin:cholesterol acyltransferase — uses phosphatidylcholine). Reference: Harper's — CETP and Lipid Transfer.

questions.questionText: Phospholipase A2 cleaves phospholipids to release:
questions.optionB: Fatty acid at sn-2 position (usually arachidonic acid) → substrate for eicosanoid synthesis
questions.explanation: Phospholipase A2 (PLA2): cleaves ester bond at sn-2 of phospholipid → releases fatty acid (usually arachidonic acid, C20:4) + lysophospholipid. Released arachidonic acid → substrate for COX (prostaglandins, thromboxanes) and LOX (leukotrienes) pathways → eicosanoid synthesis. Glucocorticoids inhibit PLA2 (via lipocortin/annexin A1) → anti-inflammatory effect. Snake venom PLA2 is a major toxin. Other phospholipases: PLA1 (sn-1), PLC (head group — generates DAG + IP3), PLD (releases head group alcohol). Reference: Harper's — Phospholipases.

questions.questionText: Krabbe disease (globoid cell leukodystrophy) is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: Galactocerebrosidase (galactosylceramidase)
questions.explanation: Krabbe disease: autosomal recessive, galactocerebrosidase deficiency → galactocerebroside (galactosylceramide) accumulates primarily in brain (white matter). Results in accumulation of psychosine (galactosylsphingosine — toxic to oligodendrocytes). Features: severe CNS demyelination, globe cell/multinucleated macrophages in brain, irritability, spasticity, blindness, death by age 2. No hepatosplenomegaly (unlike Gaucher). Metachromatic leukodystrophy (MLD): arylsulfatase A deficiency → sulfatide accumulation → also causes demyelination. Reference: Harper's — Krabbe Disease.

questions.questionText: Membrane lipid asymmetry is maintained by flippases. Phosphatidylserine is normally found on the:
questions.optionA: Outer leaflet of the plasma membrane
questions.optionB: Inner (cytoplasmic) leaflet — its exposure on the outer leaflet signals apoptosis
questions.explanation: Phospholipid membrane asymmetry: PS (phosphatidylserine) and PE (phosphatidylethanolamine): inner (cytoplasmic) leaflet. PC (phosphatidylcholine) and sphingomyelin: outer leaflet. Flippases: ATP-dependent, move PS/PE inward (maintain asymmetry). Scramblases: Ca2+-activated, randomize distribution. During apoptosis: scramblase activated, flippase inhibited → PS exposed on outer leaflet → 'eat-me' signal recognized by phagocytes (macrophages via annexin V). Annexin V staining detects apoptotic cells. Reference: Harper's — Membrane Asymmetry.

questions.questionText: Arachidonic acid is essential for eicosanoid synthesis and is a:
questions.optionB: Polyunsaturated omega-6 fatty acid with 20 carbons and 4 double bonds (C20:4, ω-6)
questions.explanation: Arachidonic acid (C20:4, Δ5,8,11,14, ω-6): most abundant precursor for eicosanoid synthesis. Derived from dietary linoleic acid (C18:2, ω-6 — essential fatty acid) through desaturation and elongation. Released from membrane phospholipids by PLA2. COX pathway: prostaglandins (PGE2, PGI2), thromboxanes (TXA2). LOX pathway: leukotrienes (LTB4, LTC4/D4/E4). EPA (C20:5, ω-3) competes with arachidonic acid → anti-inflammatory eicosanoids. NSAIDs inhibit COX. Reference: Harper's — Arachidonic Acid and Eicosanoids.

questions.questionText: Fabry disease is caused by deficiency of:
questions.optionB: α-Galactosidase A (ceramide trihexoside/globotriaosylceramide accumulation)
questions.explanation: Fabry disease: X-linked recessive (only X-linked sphingolipidosis), α-galactosidase A deficiency → globotriaosylceramide (Gb3/ceramide trihexoside) accumulation in blood vessel endothelium, kidney, heart, nervous system. Features: angiokeratomas (dark red skin lesions), acroparesthesias (burning pain in hands/feet — earliest symptom), corneal dystrophy (cornea verticillata), renal failure, cardiac disease. One of the few sphingolipidoses with relatively normal intellect. Enzyme replacement therapy available (agalsidase). Reference: Harper's — Fabry Disease.

questions.questionText: Essential fatty acids are those that cannot be synthesized by the body and must be obtained from diet. These include:
questions.optionB: Linoleic acid (ω-6) and α-linolenic acid (ω-3)
questions.explanation: Essential fatty acids (EFAs): humans lack Δ12 and Δ15 desaturases → cannot introduce double bonds beyond C9 from the carboxyl end. Linoleic acid (C18:2, ω-6): parent ω-6 FA → elongated/desaturated → arachidonic acid (C20:4). α-Linolenic acid (C18:3, ω-3): parent ω-3 FA → elongated/desaturated → EPA (C20:5) → DHA (C22:6). EFA deficiency: scaly dermatitis, alopecia, poor wound healing, thrombocytopenia, growth retardation. Arachidonic acid is conditionally essential (essential only if linoleic acid is deficient). Reference: Harper's — Essential Fatty Acids.

questions.questionText: Docosahexaenoic acid (DHA, 22:6 ω-3) is concentrated in the brain and retina. Its unique biochemical property is:
questions.optionA: It is a saturated fatty acid
questions.optionB: DHA's 6 double bonds create extreme membrane fluidity essential for photoreceptor disc membrane function (rapid rhodopsin conformational changes) and synaptic vesicle cycling; DHA is the most unsaturated FA in brain phospholipids
questions.optionC: It is synthesized entirely in the brain
questions.optionD: It functions primarily as an energy source
questions.explanation: DHA — brain and retinal biochemistry: DHA (22:6 ω-3): synthesized from α-linolenic acid (ALA, 18:3 ω-3) through elongation + desaturation (Δ6, Δ5 desaturases) + one round of peroxisomal β-oxidation (24:6 → 22:6). DHA comprises ~50% of retinal photoreceptor membrane FA and ~40% of brain grey matter membrane FA. Functions: (1) Extreme FLUIDITY: 6 cis double bonds → highly kinked → prevents tight packing → most fluid membrane possible → essential for RHODOPSIN conformational changes (visual cycle requires rapid disc membrane deformation), (2) Synaptic vesicle formation and cycling, (3) Neuroprotectin D1 (NPD1): anti-inflammatory/neuroprotective resolvin from DHA, (4) Gene regulation: DHA activates RXR, PPARs. Deficiency in infancy → impaired visual and cognitive development. Breast milk DHA content varies with maternal diet. Reference: Harper's — DHA Brain.

questions.questionText: A defect in the synthesis of specialized very long-chain fatty acids (VLCFAs) and their incorporation into sphingolipids predominantly affects which of the following cellular components?
questions.optionB: Myelin sheath of neurons
questions.explanation: Very long-chain fatty acids (VLCFAs) and sphingolipids (such as galactocerebroside and sphingomyelin) are essential structural components of the myelin sheath in the central and peripheral nervous systems. Defects in long-chain fatty acid metabolism or sphingolipid synthesis frequently lead to demyelinating diseases (leukodystrophies).

questions.questionText: A 6-year-old child has bulky foul-smelling stools, failure to thrive, and prolonged PT. Stool fat is elevated. Which sequence best explains normal intestinal lipid absorption that is disrupted in this child?
questions.optionA: Chylomicron assembly -> bile salt secretion -> pancreatic lipase action -> micelle formation -> enterocyte uptake
questions.optionB: Bile salt emulsification -> pancreatic lipase-colipase hydrolysis -> mixed micelle formation -> enterocyte uptake -> re-esterification and ApoB-48 chylomicron assembly -> lymphatic transport
questions.optionC: Pancreatic phospholipase only -> portal transport of triglycerides -> hepatic chylomicron release
questions.optionD: Gastric lipase hydrolysis -> LDL packaging in enterocyte -> portal transport
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B because physiological fat handling is: emulsification by bile salts, hydrolysis by pancreatic lipase with colipase, micellar solubilization, enterocyte uptake, re-esterification, ApoB-48-dependent chylomicron assembly, then lymphatic transport. Why others are wrong: A reverses the sequence, C ignores major lipase and ApoB-48 pathway, D incorrectly uses LDL/portal route for dietary TG. Key principle: bile salts + pancreatic enzymes + ApoB-48 are all required for efficient long-chain fat absorption. Clinical significance: disruption causes steatorrhea and fat-soluble vitamin deficiency (A, D, E, K), with prolonged PT from vitamin K deficiency.

questions.questionText: Which statement correctly differentiates chylomicrons from VLDL in an exam-trap comparison?
questions.optionA: Both contain ApoB-100 as their structural apoprotein
questions.optionB: Chylomicrons carry exogenous triglycerides from intestine and contain ApoB-48; VLDL carries endogenous triglycerides from liver and contains ApoB-100
questions.optionC: VLDL enters lacteals while chylomicrons are secreted directly into portal blood
questions.optionD: Only chylomicrons are hydrolyzed by lipoprotein lipase
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. Chylomicrons are intestinal, exogenous-TG particles with ApoB-48; VLDL is hepatic, endogenous-TG particle with ApoB-100. Why others are wrong: A ignores ApoB-48/ApoB-100 distinction, C reverses transport routes, D is false because LPL hydrolyzes TG from both chylomicrons and VLDL. Key differentiating clue: ApoB-48 is intestine-specific and identifies exogenous pathway.

questions.questionText: An infant develops seizures and lethargy after overnight fasting. Labs show hypoketotic hypoglycemia, elevated C8 acylcarnitine, and dicarboxylic aciduria. Most likely defect is:
questions.optionB: Medium-chain acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. MCAD deficiency prevents beta-oxidation of medium-chain fatty acids during fasting, causing low ATP/NADH and reduced ketogenesis (hypoketotic hypoglycemia), with characteristic medium-chain acylcarnitine accumulation and dicarboxylic aciduria from omega-oxidation backup. Why others are wrong: CPT-I deficiency has low long-chain acylcarnitines and carnitine shuttle defect pattern, HMG-CoA lyase defect primarily impairs ketone synthesis downstream, pyruvate carboxylase defect is gluconeogenic/lactic phenotype. Key pathway: mitochondrial beta-oxidation is essential for fasting adaptation.

questions.questionText: All are true regarding malonyl-CoA EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: It is generated by acetyl-CoA carboxylase in the fed state
questions.optionB: It supplies two-carbon units to fatty acid synthase
questions.optionC: It allosterically activates CPT-I to increase mitochondrial beta-oxidation
questions.optionD: Its rise provides reciprocal control by suppressing fatty acid entry into mitochondria
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C because malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT-I; it does not activate it. Why others are wrong (i.e., true): A is true (ACC product), B is true (elongation substrate in FAS), D is true (prevents futile simultaneous synthesis and oxidation). Key principle: reciprocal regulation of lipogenesis and beta-oxidation through malonyl-CoA-CPT-I axis.

questions.questionText: A teenager has progressive retinitis pigmentosa, anosmia, ataxia, and peripheral neuropathy. Plasma phytanic acid is elevated. The deficient pathway is:
questions.optionA: Mitochondrial beta-oxidation of even-chain fatty acids
questions.optionB: Peroxisomal alpha-oxidation of branched-chain fatty acids
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. Refsum disease involves defective alpha-oxidation of phytanic acid (a branched-chain fatty acid with beta-carbon methyl group blocking beta-oxidation). Why others are wrong: A does not explain phytanic acid accumulation, C is unrelated to this neuro-retinal syndrome, D is compensatory and not the primary blocked route. Key enzyme-pathway concept: alpha-oxidation in peroxisomes handles beta-oxidation-resistant branched lipids.

questions.questionText: A child with hypotonia, seizures, hepatomegaly, and demyelination has markedly elevated very long-chain fatty acids. The primary subcellular defect is:
questions.optionB: Peroxisomal beta-oxidation failure
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. VLCFAs are first shortened in peroxisomes; failure causes VLCFA accumulation and myelin toxicity, classically in peroxisomal disorders such as Zellweger spectrum and X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy patterns. Why others are wrong: A does not cause VLCFA buildup, C gives energy-failure phenotype without selective VLCFA rise, D corresponds to Niemann-Pick with different metabolite signature. Clinical clue: neuropathy/demyelination with high VLCFA points to peroxisomal oxidation defect.

questions.questionText: Ketone body utilization is absent in liver because hepatocytes lack:
questions.optionB: HMG-CoA lyase
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. Liver produces ketones (ketogenesis) but cannot oxidize them because it lacks thiophorase, required to activate acetoacetate for ketolysis. Why others are wrong: A and B are ketogenesis enzymes present in liver mitochondria, D is present and mediates beta-hydroxybutyrate/acetoacetate interconversion. Key trap: liver exports ketones to extrahepatic tissues (heart, skeletal muscle, prolonged-fasting brain) but does not consume them.
questions.conceptTag: Ketolysis thiophorase liver cannot use ketones

questions.questionText: In severe diabetic ketoacidosis, urine ketone strip positivity may appear modest initially despite marked ketosis because nitroprusside reagent primarily detects:
questions.optionB: Beta-hydroxybutyrate only
questions.optionC: Acetoacetate (and acetone weakly), but not beta-hydroxybutyrate
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. High NADH in DKA shifts ketone pool toward beta-hydroxybutyrate, which nitroprusside does not detect well; thus urine strip can underestimate severity early. Why others are wrong: A and B are incomplete/incorrect specificity claims, D is false because detection is not equivalent across ketones. Clinical significance: serum beta-hydroxybutyrate is more reliable for monitoring DKA severity.

questions.questionText: A patient has recurrent pancreatitis, eruptive xanthomas, lipemic plasma, very high triglycerides since childhood, and normal LDL-C. Most likely primary defect is:
questions.optionB: ApoE2 homozygosity
questions.optionC: ApoC-II deficiency or lipoprotein lipase deficiency
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. Severe fasting chylomicronemia with recurrent pancreatitis indicates impaired clearance of TG-rich lipoproteins due to LPL inactivity (LPL or ApoC-II defect). Why others are wrong: A gives isolated LDL rise with tendon xanthomas, B gives remnant (Type III) pattern with mixed cholesterol/TG elevation and palmar xanthomas, D causes very low HDL (Tangier). Differentiating clue: pancreatitis plus extreme TG-rich plasma from childhood.

questions.questionText: A middle-aged patient has palmar xanthomas and premature vascular disease. Lipids show elevation of both cholesterol and triglycerides due to remnant accumulation. Most likely defect is:
questions.optionB: ApoE abnormality causing defective remnant uptake
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. Type III dysbetalipoproteinemia is classically due to ApoE2-related defective hepatic clearance of chylomicron and VLDL remnants, causing mixed hyperlipidemia and palmar/tuberous xanthomas. Why others are wrong: A causes abetalipoproteinemia-like intestinal export failure pattern, C presents with corneal opacities/hemolysis and abnormal HDL maturation, D is a fatty acid oxidation disorder not a remnant disorder. Key trap: remnant uptake is ApoE-dependent, not LDL receptor-ApoB-100 pathway alone.

questions.questionText: A young adult has orange tonsils, peripheral neuropathy, hepatosplenomegaly, and extremely low HDL. Defective reverse cholesterol transport is due to mutation in:
questions.optionB: ApoC-II
questions.explanation: Correct answer is A. Tangier disease is due to ABCA1 defect, preventing cholesterol efflux from peripheral cells to ApoA-I and causing near-absent HDL. Why others are wrong: ApoC-II affects LPL activation and TG clearance, LDLR/ApoB-100 defects mainly raise LDL-C. Key pathway: ABCA1 initiates HDL biogenesis and reverse cholesterol transport.

questions.questionText: Which comparison between LCAT and ACAT is correct?
questions.optionA: Both are intracellular enzymes in hepatocyte ER
questions.optionB: LCAT is a plasma enzyme activated by ApoA-I on HDL; ACAT is intracellular and esterifies cholesterol for storage within cells
questions.optionC: ACAT is a plasma enzyme mediating reverse cholesterol transport
questions.optionD: LCAT uses acyl-CoA as fatty acyl donor in cytosol
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. LCAT is circulating, HDL-associated, and transfers fatty acid from lecithin to free cholesterol; ACAT (SOAT) is intracellular and uses acyl-CoA to esterify cholesterol for storage. Why others are wrong: A and C invert location/function, D describes ACAT chemistry, not LCAT. Differentiating clue: LCAT is lipoprotein-surface maturation; ACAT is cellular storage.

questions.questionText: All are true regarding eicosanoid pathway inhibition EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: Glucocorticoids reduce arachidonic acid release by inducing annexin-mediated phospholipase A2 inhibition
questions.optionB: Aspirin irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase
questions.optionC: Leukotriene synthesis is blocked by selective COX-2 inhibitors
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C because selective COX-2 inhibition blocks prostaglandins but not leukotriene synthesis (LOX pathway). Why others are wrong (i.e., true): A is glucocorticoid upstream effect, B is classic aspirin mechanism, D is true for 5-LOX inhibitors like zileuton. Clinical significance: aspirin-sensitive asthma can worsen when COX is blocked and arachidonic acid is diverted toward leukotrienes.

questions.questionText: A preterm neonate develops respiratory distress shortly after birth. Amniotic fluid had low lecithin:sphingomyelin ratio. The best biochemical interpretation is:
questions.optionA: Excess sphingomyelin in mature lungs increases compliance
questions.optionB: Low DPPC synthesis by type II pneumocytes leads to high alveolar surface tension and collapse
questions.optionC: High cardiolipin in surfactant directly causes hyaline membrane disease
questions.optionD: Surfactant deficiency is due to ApoB-48 absence
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. Low L:S ratio indicates fetal lung immaturity with deficient dipalmitoylphosphatidylcholine (lecithin/DPPC), causing high surface tension and atelectasis. Why others are wrong: A reverses implication of L:S ratio, C confuses mitochondrial cardiolipin with surfactant composition, D is unrelated lipoprotein biology. Clinical significance: guides antenatal steroid use and postnatal surfactant therapy.

questions.questionText: Which statement is INCORRECT regarding cis-trans unsaturation and membrane biophysics?
questions.optionA: Cis double bonds introduce kinks that reduce tight packing
questions.optionB: Trans unsaturated fatty acids pack more like saturated fatty acids
questions.optionC: Increasing cis polyunsaturation generally increases membrane fluidity
questions.optionD: Trans double bonds increase membrane fluidity more than cis double bonds of equal chain length
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D because trans unsaturation behaves more like saturated chains and does not increase fluidity the way cis kinks do. Why others are wrong (i.e., true): A, B, and C describe established structural effects of chain geometry. Key structural principle: geometry of double bonds determines packing, fluidity, and downstream signaling microdomain behavior.

questions.questionText: A patient on long-term bile acid sequestrant therapy develops easy bruising and prolonged PT. Which mechanism best explains this complication?
questions.optionA: Reduced micellar absorption of fat-soluble vitamins due to intestinal bile acid binding
questions.optionB: Direct hepatic inhibition of vitamin K epoxide reductase
questions.optionC: Increased renal loss of vitamin K
questions.optionD: Competitive inhibition of gamma-glutamyl carboxylase by statins
questions.explanation: Correct answer is A. Bile acid sequestrants reduce enterohepatic bile salt availability, impairing micellar absorption of lipids and fat-soluble vitamins, especially vitamin K, causing coagulopathy. Why others are wrong: B describes warfarin mechanism, C is not primary in this setting, D is incorrect pharmacology. Clinical significance: monitor PT/INR and supplement vitamins when indicated.
questions.conceptTag: Bile acid sequestrants vitamin K malabsorption

questions.questionText: Which apolipoprotein-enzyme pairing is correctly matched to physiologic function?
questions.optionB: ApoB-48 binds LDL receptor for peripheral uptake
questions.optionC: ApoE activates pancreatic lipase in intestinal lumen
questions.optionD: ApoA-I activates LCAT on HDL surface
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D. ApoA-I is the key activator of LCAT during HDL maturation. Why others are wrong: ApoC-II activates LPL (not HMG-CoA reductase), ApoE mediates remnant receptor recognition (not luminal digestion), ApoB-48 is structural for chylomicrons and does not bind LDL receptor like ApoB-100. Differentiating clue: ApoA-I-LCAT is central to reverse cholesterol transport.

questions.questionText: A teenager has tendon xanthomas and LDL-C 360 mg/dL with normal triglycerides. Which mechanism most directly explains accelerated atherosclerosis?
questions.optionA: Failure of ApoB-48 synthesis causing chylomicron retention
questions.optionB: Loss of ApoC-II causing high chylomicron triglycerides only
questions.optionC: Reduced LDL receptor-mediated endocytosis leading to prolonged plasma residence and arterial LDL oxidation
questions.optionD: ABCA1 loss causing isolated low HDL without LDL elevation
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. Familial hypercholesterolemia (classically LDL receptor pathway defects) causes persistent high LDL exposure, enhanced subendothelial retention/oxidation, and early plaque formation. Why others are wrong: A causes fat malabsorption/abetalipoproteinemia-type states, B is pancreatitis-predominant hypertriglyceridemia pattern, D is Tangier phenotype with low HDL. Key mechanism: LDL receptor pathway failure drives LDL burden and premature CAD.

questions.questionText: Which sequence correctly represents one cycle of mitochondrial beta-oxidation after fatty acyl-CoA enters matrix?
questions.optionB: Hydration -> oxidation (NAD+) -> thiolysis -> oxidation (FAD)
questions.explanation: Correct answer is A. The recurring order is: acyl-CoA dehydrogenase (FAD), enoyl-CoA hydratase, beta-hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase (NAD+), then beta-ketothiolase cleavage. Why others are wrong: they scramble redox and cleavage order. Key pathway principle: each round yields acetyl-CoA plus shortened acyl-CoA, with one FADH2 and one NADH.

questions.questionText: All are true regarding LDL receptor pathway versus remnant uptake pathway EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: ApoB-100 is the major ligand for classical LDL receptor-mediated LDL uptake
questions.optionB: ApoE is critical for hepatic uptake of chylomicron remnants and IDL
questions.optionC: Type III dysbetalipoproteinemia is primarily due to impaired ApoE-dependent remnant clearance
questions.optionD: Familial hypercholesterolemia is mainly caused by defective ApoE-mediated remnant binding
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D because familial hypercholesterolemia classically reflects LDL receptor/ApoB-100/PCSK9-axis defects, not primary ApoE remnant-binding defects. Why others are wrong (i.e., true): A defines LDL pathway, B and C define remnant pathway biology. Differentiating clue: isolated LDL elevation with tendon xanthomas points to LDL receptor axis; mixed remnant elevation suggests ApoE pathology.
questions.conceptTag: LDL receptor vs ApoE remnant uptake

questions.questionText: Gain-of-function mutation of PCSK9 most directly causes which lipid profile change and mechanism?
questions.optionA: Low LDL-C by increasing LDL receptor recycling
questions.optionB: Severe hypertriglyceridemia by inactivating lipoprotein lipase
questions.optionC: High LDL-C by promoting lysosomal degradation of hepatic LDL receptors
questions.optionD: Very low HDL-C by blocking ABCA1-mediated cholesterol efflux
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. PCSK9 binds LDL receptors and targets them for degradation, reducing receptor recycling and hepatic LDL clearance. Why others are wrong: A is opposite effect, B corresponds to LPL pathway defects, D corresponds to Tangier mechanism. Clinical significance: PCSK9 inhibitors lower LDL by preserving LDL receptor density.

questions.questionText: Which comparison between lipoprotein lipase (LPL) and hepatic lipase is most accurate?
questions.optionA: LPL is capillary endothelium-bound and clears TG from chylomicrons/VLDL; hepatic lipase remodels IDL/HDL at hepatic surface and does not require ApoC-II
questions.optionB: Both require ApoC-II for full activity
questions.optionC: Hepatic lipase is the main enzyme for intravascular chylomicron TG hydrolysis
questions.optionD: LPL converts free cholesterol to cholesteryl ester on HDL
questions.explanation: Correct answer is A. LPL (ApoC-II dependent) hydrolyzes circulating TG-rich particles in peripheral capillaries; hepatic lipase acts mainly at liver sinusoidal surface in remnant/HDL remodeling and is ApoC-II independent. Why others are wrong: B overgeneralizes ApoC-II dependence, C misassigns chylomicron clearance enzyme, D describes LCAT. Differentiating clue: LPL = peripheral TG unloading; hepatic lipase = hepatic remodeling.

questions.questionText: Which statement best integrates omega nomenclature with essential fatty acid biology?
questions.optionA: Omega number counts from the carboxyl end; humans can introduce omega-3 double bonds de novo
questions.optionB: Linoleic acid is omega-3 and alpha-linolenic acid is omega-6
questions.optionC: Omega number counts from methyl end; linoleic acid (18:2 omega-6) and alpha-linolenic acid (18:3 omega-3) are dietary essentials because humans lack delta12/delta15 desaturases
questions.optionD: Arachidonic acid is absolutely essential in all diets irrespective of linoleate status
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. Omega notation counts from methyl end and explains omega-6 vs omega-3 families. Humans cannot create double bonds beyond delta9, hence linoleic and alpha-linolenic acids are essential. Why others are wrong: A misstates counting and synthetic capacity, B swaps omega families, D ignores that arachidonic acid is conditionally essential when linoleate is inadequate. Key trap: linoleic is omega-6; alpha-linolenic is omega-3.

questions.questionText: During apoptosis, phosphatidylserine exposure on outer leaflet is primarily due to:
questions.optionB: Inhibition of flippase with activation of Ca2+-dependent scramblase
questions.optionC: Selective activation of phospholipase C only
questions.explanation: Correct answer is B. Loss of asymmetry in apoptosis occurs when inward flippase activity falls and scramblase randomizes phospholipids, externalizing phosphatidylserine as an eat-me signal. Why others are wrong: A would preserve asymmetry, C and D are unrelated to leaflet redistribution. Key differentiating clue: flippase maintains asymmetry; scramblase collapses it.

questions.questionText: A child with abetalipoproteinemia has progressive neuropathy and acanthocytosis. Which vitamin deficiency most directly explains membrane oxidative injury in this condition?
questions.optionB: Vitamin D
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. In ApoB-containing lipoprotein assembly defects, fat-soluble vitamin transport is impaired; vitamin E deficiency causes unchecked lipid peroxidation in neuronal and RBC membranes, contributing to neuropathy and acanthocytosis. Why others are wrong: A primarily affects vision/epithelium, D causes coagulopathy, B affects calcium-bone axis. Key integration: lipid transport defects can present as antioxidant membrane injury.

questions.questionText: Which statement about vitamin A transport and lipid pathways is most accurate?
questions.optionA: Dietary retinyl esters are absorbed independent of micelles
questions.optionB: Vitamin A is carried only by LDL after intestinal absorption
questions.optionC: Newly absorbed retinyl esters are packaged into chylomicrons for delivery, linking vitamin A absorption to normal lipid assimilation
questions.optionD: Bile salt deficiency does not affect vitamin A uptake
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. Intestinal vitamin A absorption is fat-dependent and micelle-dependent, then retinyl esters are incorporated into chylomicrons. Why others are wrong: A and D ignore bile/micelle dependence, B is too restrictive and incorrect for initial transport. Clinical significance: steatorrhea or bile acid deficiency predisposes to vitamin A deficiency manifestations.
questions.conceptTag: Vitamin A chylomicron transport bile-dependent absorption

questions.questionText: During prolonged fasting, increased omega-oxidation with dicarboxylic aciduria most strongly suggests primary overload or failure of:
questions.optionB: Cytosolic fatty acid synthase pathway
questions.explanation: Correct answer is A. Omega-oxidation in smooth ER acts as a backup route and becomes prominent when mitochondrial beta-oxidation is impaired (for example MCAD defects), generating dicarboxylic acids. Why others are wrong: B is anabolic and unrelated, C does not produce dicarboxylic aciduria, D affects bile chemistry not this urinary signature. Key clue: fasting intolerance plus dicarboxylic aciduria indicates beta-oxidation failure.

questions.questionText: Which sequence is correct for ketone body synthesis from mitochondrial acetyl-CoA in liver?
questions.optionB: Acetoacetate -> HMG-CoA -> acetoacetyl-CoA -> beta-hydroxybutyrate
questions.explanation: Correct answer is A. Thiolase forms acetoacetyl-CoA, mitochondrial HMG-CoA synthase forms HMG-CoA, HMG-CoA lyase generates acetoacetate, then reduction gives beta-hydroxybutyrate or spontaneous decarboxylation gives acetone. Why others are wrong: B reverses direction, C mixes lipogenesis intermediate malonyl-CoA, D incorrectly inserts citrate route and ignores major products. Key pathway: fasting insulin-low state drives hepatic ketogenesis.

questions.questionText: In cholesterol biosynthesis, the rate-limiting committed step targeted by statins is:
questions.optionB: Lanosterol -> cholesterol
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D. HMG-CoA reductase in ER catalyzes the NADPH-dependent formation of mevalonate and is the key rate-limiting control point inhibited by statins. Why others are wrong: A is upstream condensation, B and C are downstream steps not principal control site. Clinical significance: reducing hepatic cholesterol pool upregulates LDL receptors and lowers plasma LDL-C.

questions.questionText: A lipid profile shows very high LDL-C with normal triglycerides and tendon xanthomas; another patient shows elevated LDL-C plus elevated triglycerides without chylomicronemia. The likely Fredrickson types are:
questions.optionA: Type IIa and Type IIb respectively
questions.optionB: Type I and Type III respectively
questions.optionC: Type III and Type IV respectively
questions.optionD: Type IIb and Type IIa respectively
questions.explanation: Correct answer is A. Type IIa is isolated LDL elevation (familial hypercholesterolemia pattern), while Type IIb has combined LDL plus VLDL elevation (high cholesterol and triglycerides). Why others are wrong: B/C mismatch classic phenotypes, D reverses the two patterns. Differentiating clue: normal TG with tendon xanthoma favors IIa.
questions.conceptTag: Type IIa vs IIb lipid pattern

questions.questionText: Which statement is FALSE regarding sphingolipidosis clinical comparison?
questions.optionA: Tay-Sachs often has cherry-red spot with neurodegeneration and usually no hepatosplenomegaly
questions.optionB: Niemann-Pick type A may show hepatosplenomegaly and cherry-red spot
questions.optionC: Fabry disease is X-linked and can present with angiokeratomas and acroparesthesias
questions.optionD: Gaucher disease classically presents with hexosaminidase A deficiency
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D because Gaucher is due to glucocerebrosidase deficiency, not hexosaminidase A. Why others are wrong (i.e., true): A, B, and C are classic exam associations. Differentiating clue: cherry-red with no organomegaly points to Tay-Sachs; organomegaly with cherry-red suggests Niemann-Pick.

questions.questionText: Why is cholesterol not a significant fuel molecule in humans?
questions.optionB: Cholesterol is not absorbed from diet
questions.optionC: Humans lack pathways to cleave and fully oxidize the steroid nucleus to acetyl-CoA/CO2
questions.optionD: It is rapidly excreted unchanged by kidneys
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. The rigid sterol ring system is not catabolized to energy-yielding units in humans, so cholesterol is used for membranes, steroid hormones, bile acids, and vitamin D precursor rather than as fuel. Why others are wrong: A is not the fundamental limitation, B is false, D is false because major elimination is via bile/bile acids. Mechanistic significance: explains dependence on bile acid pathway for cholesterol disposal.
questions.conceptTag: Why cholesterol is not used as fuel

questions.questionText: All are true about fed-state lipogenesis and fasting-state fatty acid oxidation regulation EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: Insulin activates acetyl-CoA carboxylase and promotes malonyl-CoA formation
questions.optionB: Glucagon/AMPK-mediated phosphorylation suppresses acetyl-CoA carboxylase
questions.optionC: Malonyl-CoA inhibits CPT-I, decreasing mitochondrial long-chain fatty acyl entry
questions.optionD: In fasting state, high malonyl-CoA facilitates maximal beta-oxidation
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D because fasting lowers malonyl-CoA, relieving CPT-I inhibition and allowing beta-oxidation. Why others are wrong (i.e., true): A-C are core reciprocal regulation principles. Key integration: hormonal state coordinates synthesis and oxidation to avoid futile cycling.

questions.questionText: Which comparison of phosphatidylcholine and sphingomyelin is correct?
questions.optionA: Phosphatidylcholine has glycerol backbone; sphingomyelin has ceramide (sphingosine + fatty acid) backbone
questions.optionB: Both are glycerophospholipids with glycerol backbone
questions.optionC: Only phosphatidylcholine contains phosphocholine head group
questions.optionD: Only sphingomyelin participates in lipoprotein structure
questions.explanation: Correct answer is A. Phosphatidylcholine is a glycerophospholipid, whereas sphingomyelin is a sphingophospholipid built on ceramide. Why others are wrong: B misclassifies sphingomyelin, C is false because both can have phosphocholine head group, D is false because phosphatidylcholine is abundant in lipoproteins and surfactant. Differentiating clue: backbone identity is the key discriminator.

questions.questionText: A patient with severe cholestasis develops night blindness, osteomalacia, neuropathy, and easy bruising. The unifying biochemical defect is:
questions.optionA: Reduced renal activation of vitamin D only
questions.optionB: Increased hepatic beta-oxidation
questions.optionD: Impaired micelle-dependent intestinal absorption of fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, K
questions.explanation: Correct answer is D. Bile flow failure reduces intestinal bile salts, impairing micelle formation and uptake of all fat-soluble vitamins, producing multisystem deficiency signs. Why others are wrong: A is too narrow, B and C do not explain combined ADEK deficiency pattern. Clinical integration: links hepatobiliary disease with lipid digestion and vitamin deficiency syndromes.

questions.questionText: Which lipoprotein transport flow is correctly ordered for endogenous pathway progression?
questions.optionB: HDL -> LDL -> VLDL
questions.optionC: VLDL -> IDL -> LDL with progressive triglyceride loss and cholesterol enrichment
questions.explanation: Correct answer is C. Liver secretes VLDL (TG-rich), lipolysis converts it to IDL, then to cholesterol-richer LDL. Why others are wrong: A is exogenous pathway, B and D are incorrect sequences. Key differentiating clue: endogenous TG export starts with VLDL, not chylomicrons.
